tweets.content:
Daily US-Israel-Iran War Brief:
16th Jul 2026:

- We will be posting daily war updates on the ongoing war in Middle East
- ⁠Follow us for daily Brief

● Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz an inviolable red line today, warning that any US strikes on Iranian infrastructure, including the power plants and bridges, would trigger retaliatory strikes against critical infrastructure across the Gulf region.
● The IDF eliminated 3 armed Hezbollah terrorists, who were carrying combat equipment, in the area of Beit Yahoun which is located within the Security Zone in southern Lebanon.

● Iran’s army said on Thursday that its drones targeted US radar systems, Patriot air defenses and fuel storage at Ali Al Salem Air Base in Kuwait, as well as communications and radar systems at Isa Air Base in Bahrain.

● Nine vessels crossed the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, down from 13 on Tuesday, with most
using the Iranian route.

● India ordered shipowners and operators not to deploy Indian seafarers on vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz until further notice.

● Iraq suspended its crude oil loading at all terminals, following a drone strike, which has been attributed to Iranian-backed non-state actors in country, on a tanker at the Basra Oil Terminal.

END OF THE BRIEF

tweets.title: Many of you asked about the Saudi strike on Sana'a Airport (Jul 13). Here is what we know so far ✅ Weapon used: Storm Shadow cruise missile, confirmed by Yemeni military and government sources, reported by many outlets ✅ Goal: stop an Iranian plane (carrying a Houthi group) from landing ✅ Result: the runway was not fully destroyed (usually they aren’t - idea is always to make them non functional) and plane landed at Hodeidah instead ✅ Saudi Arabia has not confirmed the strike; reports say it is blaming the Aden-based Yemeni government ✅ Claims of ~10 missiles fired by Saudi Eurofighters from long range come from only one source not confirmed. Will keep tracking this and share updates as more details come out
tweets.content:
Many of you asked about the Saudi strike on Sana'a Airport (Jul 13). Here is what we know so far

✅ Weapon used: Storm Shadow cruise missile, confirmed by Yemeni military and government sources, reported by many outlets
✅ Goal: stop an Iranian plane (carrying a Houthi group) from landing
✅ Result: the runway was not fully destroyed (usually they aren’t - idea is always to make them non functional) and plane landed at Hodeidah instead
✅ Saudi Arabia has not confirmed the strike; reports say it is blaming the Aden-based Yemeni government
✅ Claims of ~10 missiles fired by Saudi Eurofighters from long range come from only one source not confirmed.

Will keep tracking this and share updates as more details come out

tweets.title: Pakistan Sponsered Terror Group Lashkar-e-Taiba Is Expanding Its Training with AI & Maritime Infiltration Indian Security agencies are monitoring what they describe as an evolution in Lashkar-e-Taiba's (LeT) training, with new videos reportedly showing cadres being trained in water-based infiltration, martial arts, and artificial intelligence. Reported developments: - Maritime infiltration training at locations including Mangla Dam, where the 2008 Mumbai attackers also reportedly trained. - Close-combat training in judo, karate, taekwondo, and wrestling. - AI and technology training aimed at improving operational planning, cyber capabilities, and online propaganda. - Women are participating in several AI-related training programmes. These developments could enhance the group's capabilities in maritime operations, cyber activities, and digital propaganda, and are being closely monitored by Indian agencies.
tweets.content:
Pakistan Sponsered Terror Group Lashkar-e-Taiba Is Expanding Its Training with AI & Maritime Infiltration

Indian Security agencies are monitoring what they describe as an evolution in Lashkar-e-Taiba's (LeT) training, with new videos reportedly showing cadres being trained in water-based infiltration, martial arts, and artificial intelligence.

Reported developments:

- Maritime infiltration training at locations including Mangla Dam, where the 2008 Mumbai attackers also reportedly trained.

- Close-combat training in judo, karate, taekwondo, and wrestling.

- AI and technology training aimed at improving operational planning, cyber capabilities, and online propaganda.

- Women are participating in several AI-related training programmes.

These developments could enhance the group's capabilities in maritime operations, cyber activities, and digital propaganda, and are being closely monitored by Indian agencies.

tweets.title: BREAKING: ISRO Moves to Stop Scientist Exodus from Key Missions The Department of Space has directed ISRO centres not to routinely accept resignations or voluntary retirement requests from Group 'A' scientific and technical personnel working on Gaganyaan and other critical national missions. The move follows a reported rise in resignations, with around 100 ISRO personnel having left the organisation across various centres. The memo states that all such requests for personnel associated with strategic projects will now require Department of Space approval. The government has maintained that recruitment is underway and critical missions, including Gaganyaan, will not be allowed to suffer due to manpower shortages.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 14/14 Major Sonam Wangchuk, MVC - Ladakh Scouts  In late May 1999, as the scale of the infiltration in the Kargil sector was just becoming apparent, the Indian Army scrambled to assess the extent of the enemy's incursions. The Chorbat La sector, situated at an extreme altitude of over 18,000 feet, was a highly sensitive glaciated axis. If the enemy secured the ridge lines here, they could effectively sever the supply lines to the Siachen Glacier and open a new front for deep infiltration. Major Sonam Wangchuk, an officer of the Assam Regiment on deputation to the Indus Wing of the Ladakh Scouts, was perfectly suited for the terrain. The Ladakh Scouts, composed of local soldiers acclimated to the severe high-altitude environment, were deployed to patrol the Line of Control, establish forward observation posts, and neutralize any enemy presence in the sector. On May 26, 1999, Major Wangchuk led a patrol column consisting of a Junior Commissioned Officer (JCO) and 30 other ranks. Their mission was to secure an 18,000-foot-high ridge line on the Line of Control. The conditions were brutal, deep snow, sub-zero temperatures, and thin air that made every step a massive physical exertion. As the column navigated a narrow, glaciated approach toward the ridge, they were suddenly ambushed. Enemy forces, who had already established a dominant vantage point on the heights above, unleashed heavy automatic machine-gun and mortar fire on the patrol. The initial barrage resulted in the death of a Non-Commissioned Officer (NCO) and pinned the rest of the column down on the exposed glacier. Retreating under the sustained fire was not a viable tactical option, nor was staying stationary, which would have meant the eventual annihilation of the patrol. Demonstrating sharp combat clarity, Major Wangchuk realized that a direct frontal assault would be suicidal. Instead, he opted for a highly dangerous flanking maneuver. The Flanking Maneuver: Leaving a small element to lay down suppressive fire and hold the enemy's attention, Major Wangchuk took a handful of his men and began scaling a near-vertical, sheer ice wall under heavy enemy fire to outflank the entrenched positions. Close-Quarter Combat: Operating at the absolute limits of human endurance, Major Wangchuk and his team successfully breached the enemy's flank. He led a direct charge into the enemy sangars. Clearing the Ridge: In the ensuing brutal hand-to-hand combat, Major Wangchuk personally neutralized several enemy combatants. His men, galvanized by their commander's actions, cleared the remaining positions, forcing the surviving enemy troops to abandon their heavy weapons and flee back across the Line of Control. Major Wangchuk's immediate and aggressive counter-ambush was the first successful military response of Operation Vijay. By securing the ridge line in Chorbat La, he completely sealed off that axis to further enemy infiltration. His patrol's success stabilized the sector and prevented a potential strategic disaster that could have threatened the Siachen logistics route. He held the newly captured position for several days in extreme weather, coordinating artillery fire and repelling subsequent enemy counter-attacks until reinforcements could arrive. For his exceptional tactical leadership, presence of mind, and conspicuous gallantry in one of the highest and most unforgiving battlefields in the world, Major Sonam Wangchuk was awarded the Maha Vir Chakra (MVC). He survived the war and later retired as a Colonel, continuing to be a highly respected figure in the Indian Army and the Ladakh region.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 13/14 Naik Digendra Kumar, MVC - 2 Rajputana Rifles By mid-June 1999, the Battle of Tololing had reached a critical operational juncture. Three previous attempts to capture the feature had failed due to the entrenched positions of Pakistan's Northern Light Infantry, who held a commanding view of the Srinagar-Leh highway. The 2nd Battalion, Rajputana Rifles (2 Raj Rif) was assigned the mission to break the stalemate. During the operational briefing, Naik Digendra Kumar, a seasoned commando who had earned the moniker "Cobra" during his deployment with the Indian Peace Keeping Force (IPKF) in Sri Lanka, proposed a highly aggressive tactical approach. Instead of the standard routes, Kumar advocated for a direct assault up a near-vertical, 15,000-foot cliff face that the enemy considered impassable. Under the command of Major Vivek Gupta, an elite 10-man Ghatak assault team was formed, with Kumar designated as the Light Machine Gun (LMG) detachment commander. Their specific assignment was to spearhead the attack and neutralize the enemy's heavily fortified bunker network on the ridge line. On the night of June 12, 1999, the team initiated the climb. To scale the sheer rock face, they drove pitons into the stone and secured heavy-duty ropes, advancing over a 14-hour period in sub-zero temperatures. As the assault group neared the summit in the early hours of June 13, the element of surprise was lost. The enemy detected the advance and opened fire with medium machine guns, rocket-propelled grenades, and heavy artillery from a network of 11 interconnected bunkers. The initial barrage hit the assault group immediately, causing severe casualties. Major Vivek Gupta and several other members of the team were killed in action. Naik Digendra Kumar was struck by a bullet in his left arm, and his LMG was damaged. Despite the intense pain and severe bleeding, Kumar refused medical evacuation. He improvised a tourniquet using his turban to staunch the blood loss, salvaged a weapon and grenades from his fallen comrades, and pressed forward alone. Operating on pure adrenaline and leveraging his commando training, Kumar launched a solo assault against the fortification network. Silencing the Machine Gun: Crawling through the heavy crossfire, Kumar reached the first enemy machine-gun nest. He manually grabbed the hot barrel of the weapon to redirect its line of fire and lobbed a hand grenade through the firing slit, neutralizing the occupants. Systematic Clearance: With the primary machine gun silenced, the remaining Indian reinforcements gained the momentum needed to breach the crest. Kumar continued his advance across the ridge, systematically targeting the remaining enemy positions. In total, he deployed 18 grenades to clear and dismantle multiple bunkers across the objective area. Hand-to-Hand Engagement: In the final stages of the clearance, Kumar engaged the enemy command element in close-quarter combat, single-handedly neutralizing the remaining defenders, including the enemy commander, Major Anwar Khan. Despite sustaining five bullet wounds to his body and arm, Naik Digendra Kumar successfully hoisted the Indian Tricolour atop Point 4590 at approximately 04:00 AM on June 13, 1999. When reinforcement units reached the summit later that morning, they secured the peak and evacuated Kumar, who was found unconscious but alive next to the captured fortifications. The recapture of the Tololing heights was the first major strategic victory for the Indian Army during Operation Vijay, effectively shifting the momentum of the Kargil War. For his conspicuous gallantry, exceptional initiative under fire, and absolute disregard for personal safety, Naik Digendra Kumar was awarded the Maha Vir Chakra (MVC) on August 15, 1999. He survived his extensive injuries and subsequently retired from military service in 2005.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 12/14 Major Padmapani Acharya, MVC (Posthumous) - 2 Rajputana Rifles In late June 1999, following the successful recapture of Tololing Top, the 2nd Battalion, Rajputana Rifles (2 Raj Rif) was assigned its next critical objective in the Drass sector: the Black Rock Complex. Within this complex lay a heavily fortified feature known as Knoll. This elevated position allowed enemy forces to maintain a dominating line of sight and direct highly accurate artillery fire onto Indian supply routes and advancing troops. Major Padmapani Acharya was tasked with commanding 'A' Company for this pivotal assault on the night of June 28, 1999. The approach to Knoll was treacherous, requiring a steep vertical climb along a narrow, razor-sharp ridge completely devoid of any natural cover. The enemy had fortified the position extensively, utilizing minefields and establishing mutually supporting machine-gun posts to create an impenetrable wall of suppressive fire. As the assault commenced under the cover of darkness, the leading platoon of 'A' Company encountered intense artillery shelling and heavy automatic fire. The sheer volume of incoming fire stalled the initial advance, resulting in heavy casualties and threatening to derail the battalion's operational timeline on the exposed slope. Recognizing that a stalled offensive would lead to further attrition on the mountainside, Major Acharya took immediate tactical control of the situation to break the deadlock. Leading the Reserve: With absolute disregard for his personal safety, he mobilized his reserve platoon and led them directly through the raining artillery shells and enemy fire to reinforce the stalled advance. Sustaining Injuries: As he encouraged his men and pressed the charge up the steep rock face, Major Acharya sustained grievous injuries from the heavy barrage. The Final Push: Despite being severely wounded and heavily bleeding, he categorically refused to be evacuated or to stall the momentum of the attack. Realizing he was physically unable to advance further, he ordered his men to leave him behind and continue the final charge toward the fortifications. While his men surged forward, Major Acharya provided critical covering fire from his exposed position. Crawling toward the enemy perimeter despite his extensive wounds, he lobbed grenades into the immediate enemy bunkers, neutralizing the forward line of defense. Spurred by the tactical aggression, clear directives, and sacrifice of their company commander, the remaining troops of 'A' Company overran the positions and successfully captured the Knoll feature. Major Padmapani Acharya succumbed to his injuries on the battlefield shortly after the objective was secured. His decisive leadership broke the enemy's hold on the Black Rock Complex, paving the way for the complete recapture of the Tololing ridge line. For his exceptional courage, inspirational leadership, and supreme sacrifice in the face of overwhelming odds, Major Padmapani Acharya was posthumously awarded the Maha Vir Chakra (MVC), India's second-highest wartime military decoration.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 11/14 Captain Anuj Nayyar, MVC (Posthumous) - 17 Jat Regiment By early July 1999, the focus of the 17 Jat Regiment shifted toward the strategic heights of the Drass sector. Their objective was the capture of the Pt. 4875 complex, a dominating feature that allowed the enemy to disrupt movement on the critical Srinagar-Leh highway. Within this mountain complex lay "Pimple 2," a heavily fortified peak with steep, rocky inclines and interconnected enemy bunkers. On the night of July 6, 1999, Charlie Company was tasked with securing the feature. When the company commander was injured early in the assault, Captain Anuj Nayyar stepped forward to take operational command of the leading assault platoon. The approach to Pimple 2 offered no natural cover, forcing the platoon to advance up a narrow, exposed ridge under intense, direct sightlines from the enemy. As they closed in, the platoon was pinned down by heavy machine-gun fire originating from a series of mutually supporting enemy sangars (stone bunkers). Recognizing that staying stationary on the exposed slope would lead to the annihilation of his unit, Captain Nayyar chose immediate, aggressive counter-offensive action. The First Bunker: Leading his leading section from the front, Nayyar charged the first enemy fortification under a hail of automatic fire. He engaged the occupants in close-quarter combat, neutralizing the position and silencing the machine gun. The Second and Third Bunkers: Without pausing to regroup, Nayyar pressed the advance toward the next two fortifications. He personally led the charge into each bunker, engaging the enemy in brutal hand-to-hand combat and clearing both positions single-handedly. During the assault on the fourth and final bunker, the enemy resisted fiercely. As Captain Nayyar moved forward to clear the position, an enemy Rocket-Propelled Grenade (RPG) detonated directly near him, causing severe shrapnel injuries. Despite sustaining mortal wounds and bleeding profusely, Nayyar refused to fall back or seek medical attention. He maintained operational focus, directing his remaining men to press the assault and secure the final objective. He continued to fight until he collapsed on the battlefield, having successfully dismantled the entire enemy defensive network on the peak. Driven by the decisive actions and relentless advance of their commander, the remaining men of the 17 Jat overran the final position, completely securing Pimple 2. The capture of this feature broke the enemy's defensive line in the Pt. 4875 complex, allowing the Indian Army to consolidate its hold over the entire sector and secure the transit route below. For his tactical acumen, exceptional leadership under fire, and supreme sacrifice, Captain Anuj Nayyar was posthumously awarded the Maha Vir Chakra (MVC), India's second-highest military decoration for gallantry.

He continued to fight until he collapsed on the battlefield, having successfully dismantled the entire enemy defensive network on the peak.

Driven by the decisive actions and relentless advance of their commander, the remaining men of the 17 Jat overran the final position, completely securing Pimple 2. The capture of this feature broke the enemy's defensive line in the Pt. 4875 complex, allowing the Indian Army to consolidate its hold over the entire sector and secure the transit route below.

For his tactical acumen, exceptional leadership under fire, and supreme sacrifice, Captain Anuj Nayyar was posthumously awarded the Maha Vir Chakra (MVC), India's second-highest military decoration for gallantry.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 9/14 Captain Neikezhakuo Kenguruse, MVC (Posthumous) - ASC attached to 2 Rajputana Rifles In late June 1999, the 2nd Battalion, Rajputana Rifles (2 Raj Rif) was tasked with capturing a strategically critical enemy stronghold known as "Black Rock" in the Drass sector. Situated at an altitude of approximately 16,000 feet, this heavily fortified machine-gun post commanded the valley below and was effectively stalling the battalion’s advance. Captain Neikezhakuo Kenguruse, an officer commissioned into the Army Service Corps (ASC) and serving on attachment with 2 Raj Rif, was the commander of the battalion's elite Ghatak platoon. On the night of June 28, 1999, his commando unit was assigned the high-risk mission of neutralizing the position. The approach to the objective was highly restricted, requiring the platoon to scale a near-vertical, icy cliff face under the cover of darkness. The environmental conditions were severe, with temperatures dropping to -10°C. As the Ghatak platoon initiated the climb, they were detected. The enemy unleashed intense mortar and automatic machine-gun fire down the cliff face, causing heavy casualties and halting the commando team's momentum. Recognizing that his standard-issue mountain boots were failing to grip the sheer, freezing rock face, Captain Kenguruse took an extreme tactical measure. To secure a foothold and lead the advance, he removed his boots, continuing the grueling climb up the icy precipice completely barefoot. As he closed the final distance to the enemy sangars (fortifications), Captain Kenguruse sustained a severe gunshot wound to the abdomen. Despite heavy bleeding, extreme cold, and the physical toll of the ascent, he refused to fall back or stall the advance. He pushed through the pain, scaling the final stretch of the rock wall to breach the enemy perimeter. Upon clearing the cliff edge, he initiated a devastating solo assault: The Initial Strike: He immediately deployed a rocket launcher, firing directly into the primary enemy fortification to disorient the defenders and break their line of suppressive fire. Firefight: Advancing on the position, he engaged the surviving occupants with his rifle, neutralizing two enemy combatants at close range. Hand-to-Hand Combat: In the final stages of the breach, he engaged the remaining defenders in brutal close-quarter combat. Utilizing his commando knife, he neutralized two additional enemy soldiers before succumbing to his severe injuries on the ledge. Captain Kenguruse’s single-handed neutralization of the machine-gun nest broke the immediate tactical deadlock. By dismantling the forward defensive line, his actions allowed the remaining members of his platoon and the battalion to advance, subsequently securing the Black Rock objective. For his conspicuous gallantry, indomitable resolve, and supreme sacrifice in the face of overwhelming odds, Captain Neikezhakuo Kenguruse was posthumously awarded the Maha Vir Chakra (MVC). He holds the distinction of being the only officer from the Army Service Corps to receive India’s second-highest wartime military decoration.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 8/14 Major Vivek Gupta, MVC (Posthumous) - 2 Rajputana Rifles By early June 1999, the Tololing feature in the Drass sector had become a major tactical bottleneck. Rising to over 15,000 feet, the peak provided enemy forces with uninterrupted observation of the Srinagar-Leh highway, allowing them to direct highly accurate artillery fire onto Indian convoys. Several initial attempts to dislodge the entrenched defenders had stalled due to the sheer, exposed inclines and devastating crossfire. To break the deadlock, the 2nd Battalion, Rajputana Rifles (2 Raj Rif) was brought in to launch a decisive offensive. Major Vivek Gupta was assigned to command Charlie Company, tasked with spearheading the final assault on Tololing Top. On the night of June 13, 1999, Major Gupta led his company up the freezing, near-vertical slopes. The approach offered zero cover. The moment the company broke the ridgeline, they were met with a wall of heavy artillery and automatic fire from mutually supporting enemy bunkers. The intense barrage pinned the forward elements of Charlie Company down. Operating in sub-zero temperatures and thin air, any prolonged stall on the exposed rock face threatened the entire unit with heavy casualties and mission failure. Recognizing that the only way to survive was to close the distance, Major Gupta took immediate offensive action. Under continuous suppressing fire, Major Gupta rallied his lead section and initiated a direct frontal charge against the entrenched positions. The First Strike: He personally engaged the primary enemy bunker. Firing a rocket launcher to disorient the defenders, he immediately charged the fortification, engaging the occupants in close-quarter combat and neutralizing the position. Sustaining Heavy Fire: During this initial breach, Major Gupta was struck by two bullets. Despite the severity of his wounds and heavy blood loss, he refused to fall back or allow the momentum of the assault to break. The Second Bunker: Maintaining the offensive, he led his men toward the next bunker complex. He closed in on the position and engaged in brutal hand-to-hand combat, eliminating more enemy combatants. It was during this final push that he sustained additional, fatal gunshot wounds. Before succumbing to his injuries on the battlefield, Major Gupta's calculated aggression and refusal to stall under fire successfully dismantled the enemy's forward defensive line. Witnessing their company commander's decisive actions, the remaining soldiers of Charlie Company were highly galvanized. They pressed the advantage, overrunning the remaining bunkers and clearing the peak. By the morning of June 14, Tololing Top was fully secured by 2 Raj Rif. The capture of Tololing was a critical turning point in the Kargil War, initiating a domino effect that led to the subsequent recapture of several adjacent peaks. For his tactical leadership, exceptional courage, and supreme sacrifice, Major Vivek Gupta was posthumously awarded the Maha Vir Chakra (MVC), India's second-highest military decoration.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 7/14 Major Rajesh Singh Adhikari, MVC (Posthumous) - 18 Grenadiers In May 1999, the Indian Army faced the daunting task of clearing entrenched Pakistani intruders from the heights of the Drass sector. One of the most critical early objectives of the Kargil War was the Tololing feature. Its capture was essential to removing the immediate threat to the Srinagar-Leh highway and regaining tactical momentum. Major Rajesh Singh Adhikari, originally commissioned into the 2nd Battalion, Mechanised Infantry, was serving on a cross-attachment with the 18 Grenadiers. On May 30, 1999, he was tasked with leading his company to secure an initial foothold on a forward spur of Tololing, a vital step toward eventually capturing Point 4590. The approach to the objective was brutal. Major Adhikari led his men up a treacherous, near-vertical mountain face covered in snow, operating at an altitude of approximately 15,000 feet. The extreme cold and thin air compounded the difficulty of the ascent. As the company neared the forward spur, they were ambushed by intense fire. The enemy was well-fortified in mutually supporting bunkers equipped with Universal Machine Guns (UMGs). The heavy, sustained fire effectively pinned down the advancing Indian troops on the exposed mountainside. Recognizing the tactical deadlock and the vulnerability of his men, Major Adhikari initiated an immediate counter-assault to break the ambush. Engaging the First Bunker: He directed his Rocket Launcher detachment to lay down suppressive fire on one of the enemy positions. Simultaneously, Adhikari personally charged the other bunker. Breaking into the fortification, he engaged the defenders in close-quarter combat, eliminating two enemy personnel and silencing the position. Establishing Covering Fire: To maintain momentum, he ordered his Medium Machine Gun (MMG) detachment to deploy behind a rocky outcrop. This provided critical covering fire, allowing his assault party to inch further up the steep incline toward the remaining defenses. During the intense firefight to secure the perimeter, Major Adhikari sustained grievous bullet injuries. Despite severe bleeding and the extreme physical toll of the altitude, he categorically refused to be evacuated. He continued to issue orders and direct the tactical movement of his sub-unit from the front. Determined to dismantle the remaining enemy resistance, Major Adhikari led a direct charge against the second primary enemy bunker. He breached the position under heavy, concentrated fire. He neutralized one more enemy occupant in close combat, ensuring the complete capture of the bunker complex. This decisive action broke the enemy's forward defensive line on the spur. However, shortly after securing the position, Major Rajesh Singh Adhikari succumbed to his injuries on the battlefield. Major Adhikari’s refusal to halt the advance, even when mortally wounded, secured the crucial initial foothold on the Tololing feature. His tactical aggression and neutralization of the forward bunkers inflicted heavy casualties on the enemy and forced them to withdraw from their defensive perimeter. This success directly facilitated the subsequent capture of Point 4590 by Indian forces, marking a major turning point in the Battle of Tololing. For his exceptional leadership, supreme sacrifice, and conspicuous gallantry in the face of overwhelming odds, Major Rajesh Singh Adhikari was posthumously awarded the Maha Vir Chakra (MVC), India's second-highest wartime military decoration.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 6/14 Lieutenant Balwan Singh, MVC - 18 Grenadiers By early July 1999, capturing Tiger Hill had become a primary operational objective for the Indian Army. Standing at over 16,500 feet in the Drass sector, the peak offered Pakistani forces a panoramic vantage point to direct accurate artillery fire onto National Highway 1A, the critical supply route connecting Srinagar to Leh. The task of neutralizing this stronghold was assigned to the 18 Grenadiers. Lieutenant Balwan Singh, who had been commissioned into the Army just four months prior in March 1999, was the commander of the battalion's Ghatak platoon—a specialized, heavily armed infantry shock troop unit tasked with spearheading the assault. The operational plan for Tiger Hill involved a multi-pronged offensive. Lieutenant Balwan Singh and his Ghatak platoon were assigned the most treacherous route: a direct assault from the North-Eastern direction. The Terrain: The designated path was not a standard climbing route. It consisted of a near-vertical, snowbound cliff face interspersed with deep crevasses and sheer drops. It was chosen specifically because the enemy considered it impassable, providing the Indian troops with a critical opportunity for a surprise attack. The Ascent: Operating under intense, freezing conditions and continuous enemy artillery shelling, Lt. Singh led his men on a grueling 12-hour climb. The platoon utilized specialized cliff assault mountaineering equipment, driving pitons into the ice and rock to slowly advance up the precipice in near-complete silence. Before commencing the climb, Lt. Singh reportedly told his men, "Tiger Hill pe Tiranga fahrake ayenge, chahe kuchh bhi ho jaye" (We will hoist the Tricolour atop Tiger Hill, come what may). After navigating the vertical rock face for half a day, the Ghatak platoon successfully breached the summit's perimeter. The sudden appearance of Indian troops from a sheer cliff face took the entrenched Northern Light Infantry (NLI) troops completely by surprise. However, the initial shock quickly gave way to panic and a desperate, heavy counter-attack by the entrenched defenders. Sustaining Injuries: During the intense initial exchange of automatic fire and rocket-propelled grenades, Lieutenant Balwan Singh sustained severe gunshot wounds to his chest and legs. Refusing Evacuation: Despite significant blood loss and the extreme altitude, he categorically refused medical evacuation. Realizing that momentum was critical to prevent the enemy from regrouping, he continued to direct his platoon's suppressive fire. The Final Push: Unmindful of his severe injuries, Lt. Singh swiftly maneuvered to encircle the enemy fortifications. Leading from the front, he engaged the defenders in brutal hand-to-hand combat inside their bunkers, single-handedly neutralizing four enemy soldiers. The sheer ferocity and unexpected direction of the Ghatak assault, compounded by Lt. Singh's relentless close-quarter engagement despite his injuries, broke the enemy's resolve. The remaining Pakistani personnel abandoned their positions and fled the summit. The decisive actions of the Ghatak platoon under Lt. Singh's command paved the way for the 18 Grenadiers to fully consolidate their hold on Tiger Hill. The capture of the peak on July 4 effectively blinded the Pakistani artillery observers in the sector and marked a major turning point in Operation Vijay. For his tactical acumen, exceptional leadership under fire, and absolute disregard for personal safety, Lieutenant Balwan Singh was awarded the Maha Vir Chakra (MVC), India's second-highest military decoration for gallantry. He survived his injuries, remained in the Indian Army, and subsequently rose to the rank of Colonel, commanding the same 18 Grenadiers battalion later in his career.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 5/14 Rifleman Sanjay Kumar, PVC - 13 J&K Rifles By early July 1999, the Indian Army's campaign to clear the Mushkoh Valley was heavily focused on Point 4875. The capture of this high-altitude feature was essential to eliminate the enemy's dominance over the valley and secure the transit routes below. Within this complex, a highly fortified objective known as "Flat Top" stood in the way of the 13th Battalion, Jammu and Kashmir Rifles (13 JAK RIF). On July 4, the battalion initiated its assault. Rifleman Sanjay Kumar was designated as the leading scout of the attacking column. His role was to navigate the unforgiving terrain, identify enemy positions, and pave the way for the rest of the unit following closely behind. As the column advanced up the steep, bare incline, they were suddenly pinned down by intense, sustained automatic fire. The enemy had entrenched themselves in sangars, heavily fortified defensive positions constructed from boulders and rocks—that provided a clear line of sight and robust cover against small arms fire. With the advance completely stalled and his unit taking heavy fire, Rifleman Kumar recognized that the column was vulnerable to being wiped out on the exposed slope. Displaying sharp tactical awareness, he decided to break the deadlock. Under the limited covering fire of his comrades, Kumar broke from cover and charged directly toward the first enemy sangar. During this frontal assault, he was struck by two bullets, one in the chest and another in the forearm. Despite bleeding profusely from his wounds, Rifleman Kumar refused to halt his momentum. He closed the distance to the first sangar and engaged the enemy in brutal hand-to-hand combat. Overpowering the defenders, he neutralized three enemy soldiers and cleared the fortification. Realizing that the second sangar was still laying down heavy fire on his platoon, Kumar did not wait for reinforcements. Demonstrating immense physical endurance, he picked up a captured enemy Universal Machine Gun (UMG) from the cleared bunker. Bleeding and operating on pure adrenaline, he charged the second enemy position. The sheer aggression and suddenness of a wounded soldier charging with their own weapon completely disoriented the enemy defenders. By early July 1999, the Indian Army's campaign to clear the Mushkoh Valley was heavily focused on Point 4875. The capture of this high-altitude feature was essential to eliminate the enemy's dominance over the valley and secure the transit routes below. Within this complex, a highly fortified objective known as "Flat Top" stood in the way of the 13th Battalion, Jammu and Kashmir Rifles (13 JAK RIF).

On July 4, the battalion initiated its assault. Rifleman Sanjay Kumar was designated as the leading scout of the attacking column. His role was to navigate the unforgiving terrain, identify enemy positions, and pave the way for the rest of the unit following closely behind.

As the column advanced up the steep, bare incline, they were suddenly pinned down by intense, sustained automatic fire. The enemy had entrenched themselves in sangars, heavily fortified defensive positions constructed from boulders and rocks—that provided a clear line of sight and robust cover against small arms fire.

With the advance completely stalled and his unit taking heavy fire, Rifleman Kumar recognized that the column was vulnerable to being wiped out on the exposed slope. Displaying sharp tactical awareness, he decided to break the deadlock.

Under the limited covering fire of his comrades, Kumar broke from cover and charged directly toward the first enemy sangar. During this frontal assault, he was struck by two bullets, one in the chest and another in the forearm.

Despite bleeding profusely from his wounds, Rifleman Kumar refused to halt his momentum. He closed the distance to the first sangar and engaged the enemy in brutal hand-to-hand combat. Overpowering the defenders, he neutralized three enemy soldiers and cleared the fortification.

Realizing that the second sangar was still laying down heavy fire on his platoon, Kumar did not wait for reinforcements. Demonstrating immense physical endurance, he picked up a captured enemy Universal Machine Gun (UMG) from the cleared bunker.

Bleeding and operating on pure adrenaline, he charged the second enemy position. The sheer aggression and suddenness of a wounded soldier charging with their own weapon completely disoriented the enemy defenders. They abandoned their heavy machine gun and attempted to flee the position, but Kumar engaged them with the captured weapon, neutralizing the fleeing combatants and securing the second sangar.

Even after securing the critical positions, Rifleman Kumar refused medical evacuation until the rest of his platoon moved up and firmly secured the objective at Flat Top. His decisive, independent action effectively broke the enemy's defensive perimeter, allowing the 13 JAK RIF to press their advantage and eventually capture Point 4875.

Evacuated for his severe injuries, Rifleman Sanjay Kumar survived the battle. For his exceptional initiative, tactical aggression, and disregard for personal safety in the face of overwhelming odds, he was awarded the Param Vir Chakra.

tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 4/14 Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey, PVC (Posthumous) - 1/11 Gorkha Rifles In the early stages of the Kargil War, the Batalik sector presented some of the most unforgiving terrain of the conflict. The mountains were steep, the ridges were narrow, and entrenched enemy positions had established a dominating arc of fire over the valleys below. Clearing these heights required coordinated infantry assaults up sheer inclines. Captain Manoj Kumar Pandey of the 1/11 GR (1st Battalion, 11th Regiment) had already demonstrated sharp tactical maneuvering by pushing back intruders at Jubar Top. His unit's next objective was the strategically critical Khalubar ridge, a position vital to breaking the enemy's defensive line in the sector. On the night of July 2, 1999, Captain Pandey was tasked with leading his platoon to clear a heavily fortified enemy stronghold on Khalubar. The advance required moving up a completely exposed ridgeline. As the platoon closed in on the objective, they were suddenly subjected to heavy, enfilading fire from surrounding enemy positions. The crossfire effectively pinned the unit down, halting their momentum and threatening severe casualties if they remained stationary on the exposed slope. Recognizing the tactical deadlock, Captain Pandey took immediate offensive action to break the ambush. He directed one section of his platoon to flank and engage the enemy positions on the right, while he personally led an assault team to clear the bunkers directly ahead. The Initial Breach: He charged the first enemy bunker, engaged in close-quarter combat, and neutralized two enemy soldiers, breaking the initial line of defense. In the final moments of clearing the fourth position, Captain Pandey was fatally struck in the forehead by a medium machine-gun burst. Before collapsing, Captain Pandey’s final directive to his men was "Na Chhodnu" (Don't spare them). Spurred by their commander's actions, the remaining soldiers of the platoon charged the remaining positions and successfully captured Khalubar. Captain Pandey’s calculated aggression and refusal to stall under fire were decisive in collapsing the enemy's hold on the Batalik sector. tweets.title: R to @NetramDefence: 3/14 Grenadier Yogendra Singh Yadav, PVC - 18 Grenadiers In early July 1999, the focus of the Kargil War shifted heavily toward Tiger Hill, a towering, snow-covered peak dominating the Drass sector. From its summit, Pakistani forces had clear sightlines to target Indian supply lines. Recapturing it was critical, and the task fell to the 18 Grenadiers. Nineteen-year-old Grenadier Yogendra Singh Yadav was part of a select 15-man commando platoon, the Ghatak team, tasked with paving the way for the main battalion. Their mission was to scale a near-vertical, 100-foot wall of solid ice in pitch darkness, completely bypassing the heavily defended standard routes. Yadav, chosen for his agility and endurance, volunteered to lead the climb. He scaled the freezing rock face, driving pitons into the ice and anchoring ropes so the rest of the team could follow. When the team neared the top, the element of surprise was broken. The enemy spotted the climbers and opened fire with machine guns, rocket-propelled grenades, and mortars. The platoon commander and two other soldiers were killed instantly, and the advance stalled. Yadav, caught on the exposed rock face, was hit multiple times in his groin and shoulder by shrapnel and bullets. Despite severe blood loss and immense pain, he refused to stop. Recognizing that retreat meant certain death for the men below, he crawled the remaining distance over the lip of the cliff to engage the enemy bunker directly. Upon reaching the summit ledge, Yadav encountered an enemy machine-gun nest that was pinning down his team. The First Bunker: Yadav charged the bunker under a hail of bullets. He lobbed a grenade through the slit, killing three enemy soldiers and silencing the machine gun. This action allowed the remaining members of the Ghatak platoon to ascend the ropes and secure a foothold. The Second Bunker: Moments later, a second enemy bunker opened fire. Yadav, now accompanied by two comrades, charged the position. In the ensuing hand-to-hand combat, he neutralized four enemy soldiers. During the intense fighting, Yadav sustained a total of 15 bullet wounds and multiple shrapnel injuries. An enemy soldier even fired a burst into his arm, fracturing the bone. Yadav feigned death, waiting for the right moment to strike. When the enemy soldiers moved past him to check on their positions, he used his one functional arm to pull a grenade, pull the pin with his teeth, and hurl it at them. The explosion disoriented the enemy, allowing the rest of the Indian reinforcements to overrun the position. Survival and the Param Vir Chakra Believed dead by both sides due to the sheer volume of his injuries, Yadav managed to crawl down the hill to relay critical information about enemy troop strength and positions to his commanding officer. His intelligence allowed the 18 Grenadiers to adjust their strategy and fully secure Tiger Hill shortly thereafter. Against all medical odds, Yadav survived his injuries after months of intensive care. At just 19 years old, Grenadier Yogendra Singh Yadav became the youngest recipient of the Param Vir Chakra, India's highest wartime military decoration. His exceptional grit, tactical defiance under fire, and absolute refusal to surrender changed the course of the battle for Tiger Hill.

tweets.title: Dear Mr @MirwaizKashmir, Words matter. It's not the "other side of the LoC." It's "Pakistan Occupied Jammu & Kashmir". Facts don't change with terminology.
tweets.content:
Dear Mr @MirwaizKashmir,

Words matter.

It's not the "other side of the LoC." It's "Pakistan Occupied Jammu & Kashmir".

Facts don't change with terminology.

Mirwaiz Umar Farooq (@MirwaizKashmir)

Deeply saddened and pained by reports of loss of life of civilians and police personnel in the continuing unrest on the other side of LOC especially in Rawalakot and Poonch. My thoughts and prayers are with the bereaved families and all those affected by these tragic events.

I am also concerned by the ongoing discussions regarding representation and the constitutional and political status of the large number of Kashmiri State Subjects residing in Pakistan. Questions touching upon identity, representation and political rights require sensitivity, dialogue and broad public confidence.

The legitimacy and strength of any system of governance rests ultimately upon the consent, trust and confidence of the people it serves.Our history and our shared social,cultural and family ties across the Line of Control remind us that happenings in one part of Jammu and Kashmir are deeply felt in another, and among our diaspora communities
around the world.

My family has a longstanding historical associations with the social, educational, religious and political development of communities across Jammu and Kashmir, including those on the other side of LOC .

As I continue to hear expressions of anguish and distress from people across the LOC reaching out regarding the unrest, I urge the Govt of Pakistan and the local Government there, to prioritise dialogue, restraint and engagement, resolving the differences through consultation and accommodation rather than confrontation, with respect for human rights and the sanctity of human life remaining paramount.

I also appeal to protesting parties to exercise wisdom and responsibility. Both need to work towards restoring peace and public confidence at the earliest.

I pray for lasting peace and stability for all people of Jammu and Kashmir on both sides of the LOC and its resolution.

tweets.title: Su-30MKI’s radar upgrade isn’t just the AESA (Virupaksha) The radome is being redesigned too, per SoW documents. ✅ New tapered/octagonal AESA array face: 2,400 radiating elements (48×32 core + graduated taper rows) ✅ Companion radome redesign underway, aimed at improving RF transparency of the nose section ✅ Mold-line drawings show a slender ogive profile 7.5° half-angle, ~2.6m slant length Once achieved this will be the biggest AESA on any aircraft (if we go by public figures J20 was biggest so far with 2200)
tweets.content:
Su-30MKI’s radar upgrade isn’t just the AESA (Virupaksha)
The radome is being redesigned too, per SoW documents.

✅ New tapered/octagonal AESA array face: 2,400 radiating elements (48×32 core + graduated taper rows)
✅ Companion radome redesign underway, aimed at improving RF transparency of the nose section
✅ Mold-line drawings show a slender ogive profile 7.5° half-angle, ~2.6m slant length

Once achieved this will be the biggest AESA on any aircraft (if we go by public figures J20 was biggest so far with 2200)
